caseText: A 67-year-old man presents with sudden tearing chest pain radiating to his back. BP is 190/100 mmHg. BP difference between arms is noted. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Aortic dissection: sudden tearing pain radiating to BACK + hypertension + BP difference between arms. Best Dx: CT angiography. Do NOT anticoagulate.

caseText: A patient presents with palpitations and an irregularly irregular pulse. ECG shows no identifiable P waves and irregular QRS complexes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: AF: irregularly irregular rhythm + absent P waves. Most feared complication = embolic stroke. Tx: rate control (beta-blocker) + anticoagulation (DOAC preferred).

caseText: A 64-year-old man has progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, bilateral lung crackles, and pitting leg edema. BNP is elevated. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: CHF: dyspnea + orthopnea + crackles + peripheral edema + elevated BNP. Fluid overload from impaired cardiac pumping. Tx: furosemide + ACE inhibitor + beta-blocker.

caseText: Which medication has been shown to reduce mortality in systolic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction?
rationale: ACE inhibitors reduce mortality by decreasing afterload and preventing cardiac remodeling. Diuretics relieve symptoms only. Beta-blockers and spironolactone also reduce mortality.

caseText: A hemodynamically stable patient has tearing chest pain radiating to the back and BP difference between arms. What is the best initial imaging?
rationale: CT angiography: gold standard for aortic dissection. Fast and highly sensitive (98%). CXR may show widened mediastinum but is NOT diagnostic.

caseText: A 58-year-old man has chest pain only with exertion, fully relieved by rest within 5 minutes. ECG is normal at rest. Diagnosis?
rationale: Stable angina: predictable exertional chest pain relieved by rest or nitrates. Fixed coronary stenosis. Dx: stress test. Tx: beta-blocker + nitrates + aspirin + statin.

caseText: A 55-year-old man presents with crushing chest pain for 45 minutes. ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. Most appropriate immediate management?
rationale: Inferior STEMI: primary PCI is gold standard if available within 90 minutes. Give aspirin + heparin + P2Y12 inhibitor. Door-to-balloon time <90 min target.

caseText: A 25-year-old man has sharp chest pain that improves when leaning forward and worsens when lying flat. ECG shows diffuse ST elevation and PR depression. Diagnosis?
rationale: Pericarditis: sharp positional pain (better leaning forward) + diffuse ST elevation + PR depression. Friction rub on exam. Tx: NSAIDs + colchicine. Avoid in MI.

caseText: A 35-year-old woman on OCP returns from a long flight with sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and tachycardia. O2 sat 91%. Diagnosis?
rationale: PE: sudden dyspnea + pleuritic chest pain + tachycardia + risk factors (OCP + immobilization). Dx: CT pulmonary angiography. Tx: anticoagulation (LMWH/DOAC).

caseText: A 50-year-old woman has BP 210/130 mmHg with headache, blurred vision, and papilledema. What is this called?
rationale: Hypertensive emergency: severely elevated BP + end-organ damage (encephalopathy, papilledema, AKI, MI). Tx: IV labetalol or nitroprusside. Reduce BP by 20-25% in first hour.

caseText: A 70-year-old smoker has hemoptysis, 10 kg weight loss, and a 3 cm spiculated lung mass on CXR. Hoarseness is present. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Lung cancer: smoker + hemoptysis + weight loss + spiculated mass. Adenocarcinoma is #1 type overall. Small cell: central, highly aggressive, paraneoplastic syndromes.

caseText: Which type of lung cancer is most common overall, found in both smokers and non-smokers, typically in a peripheral location?
rationale: Adenocarcinoma: #1 lung cancer overall. Peripheral location. EGFR mutations targetable. Squamous cell = central, cavitating. Small cell = most aggressive, paraneoplastic, strongly associated with smoking.

caseText: A 56-year-old man has smoked 1 pack/day for 35 years and is currently smoking. What lung cancer screening is recommended?
rationale: Annual low-dose CT: age 50-80, ≥20 pack-years, currently smoking or quit <15 years. Reduces lung cancer mortality ~20%. CXR is NOT adequate for screening.

caseText: A 65-year-old smoker has chronic productive cough for >3 months over 2 consecutive years. FEV1/FVC ratio is 0.60 post-bronchodilator. Diagnosis?
rationale: COPD: FEV1/FVC <0.70 post-bronchodilator. Chronic productive cough in a smoker. Treatment: smoking cessation (most important), SABA, LABA, LAMA, ICS for frequent exacerbations.

caseText: A 60-year-old with fever 39°C, productive cough, pleuritic chest pain, and CXR showing right lower lobe consolidation. Diagnosis?
rationale: CAP: fever + cough + consolidation on CXR. Streptococcus pneumoniae = #1 cause. Tx: amoxicillin (outpatient) or azithromycin + beta-lactam (inpatient). Use CURB-65 to assess severity.

caseText: A tall thin 22-year-old man has sudden onset pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. Exam shows decreased breath sounds on the left and tracheal deviation to the right. Diagnosis?
rationale: Tension pneumothorax: sudden dyspnea + absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side. EMERGENCY: needle decompression 2nd ICS midclavicular line, then chest tube.

caseText: A 20-year-old asthmatic presents with severe dyspnea and wheezing. O2 sat 88%. Peak flow is 40% of predicted. First-line management?
rationale: Severe asthma: SABA + ipratropium nebulization + systemic corticosteroids. O2 to maintain sat >92%. Magnesium sulfate IV if life-threatening. Prepare for intubation if no response.

caseText: A 38-year-old immigrant has 4-week cough, fever, night sweats, and 10 kg weight loss. CXR shows upper lobe cavitary lesion. Diagnosis?
rationale: TB: cough >3 weeks + constitutional symptoms + upper lobe cavity. Dx: sputum AFB smear x3 + culture. Tx: RIPE x2 months then RI x4 months. Isolate until smear-negative.

caseText: A 65-year-old man has progressive dyspnea. CXR shows blunting of right costophrenic angle. Thoracentesis: protein 4.5 g/dL, LDH 250 U/L, serum LDH 200 U/L. What type of effusion?
rationale: Exudative by Light's criteria: pleural LDH/serum LDH >0.6. Exudative causes: pneumonia, malignancy, TB, PE. Transudative: CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome.

caseText: A 35-year-old woman has progressive dyspnea, syncope on exertion, and loud P2 sound. ECHO shows RV dilation and tricuspid regurgitation. Diagnosis?
rationale: Pulmonary arterial hypertension: young woman + progressive dyspnea + syncope + loud P2 + RV dilation. Dx: right heart catheterization (gold standard). Tx: endothelin antagonists, PDE5 inhibitors.

caseText: A 22-year-old has periumbilical pain migrating to RLQ over 12 hours, fever 38.4°C, and rebound at McBurney point. WBC 14,000. Diagnosis?
rationale: Appendicitis: periumbilical → RLQ migration + rebound at McBurney point + fever + leukocytosis. Tx: laparoscopic appendectomy. CT confirms if diagnosis unclear.

caseText: A 45-year-old obese woman has RUQ pain after eating a fatty meal, fever 38.2°C, nausea, and positive Murphy sign. Diagnosis?
rationale: Acute cholecystitis: RUQ pain + fever + Murphy sign + fatty meal trigger. Dx: Ultrasound (gallstones + GB wall thickening). Tx: IV fluids + antibiotics + cholecystectomy.

caseText: A 50-year-old alcoholic has epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting. Lipase is 4x upper limit of normal. Best initial treatment?
rationale: Acute pancreatitis: aggressive IV fluid resuscitation is the most important initial treatment. NPO + analgesia. Antibiotics only if infected necrosis. Causes: Gallstones #1, Alcohol #2.

caseText: A 68-year-old man has painless progressive jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, and 12 kg weight loss over 3 months. No abdominal pain. Diagnosis?
rationale: Painless jaundice + weight loss in elderly = pancreatic head cancer. Courvoisier sign: palpable non-tender gallbladder. Dx: CT abdomen. CA 19-9 elevated.

caseText: A 60-year-old woman has RUQ pain, fever 39°C, jaundice (Charcot triad), hypotension, and confusion. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Reynolds pentad = Charcot triad + hypotension + AMS = severe ascending cholangitis. Life-threatening. Tx: IV antibiotics immediately + urgent ERCP for biliary decompression.

caseText: A 60-year-old man has black tarry stools and lightheadedness. He takes ibuprofen daily. Melena indicates bleeding from which location?
rationale: Melena = digested blood = upper GI source above ligament of Treitz. NSAID use → peptic ulcer = #1 cause. Dx: urgent endoscopy. Tx: IV PPI + endoscopic hemostasis.

caseText: A 45-year-old has burning epigastric pain relieved by food. H. pylori breath test is positive. First-line treatment?
options: Triple therapy: PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin x14 days
rationale: H. pylori eradication: PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin x14 days (triple therapy). Confirm eradication 4 weeks after treatment with urea breath test or stool antigen.

caseText: An average-risk 50-year-old asks about colorectal cancer screening. What is the recommended approach in Canada?
options: Fecal immunochemical test (FIT) every 1-2 years starting age 50
No screening needed until symptoms appear
rationale: Canadian guidelines: FIT (fecal immunochemical test) every 1-2 years for average-risk adults aged 50-74. Positive FIT → colonoscopy. Average-risk colonoscopy every 10 years is also acceptable.

caseText: A 55-year-old alcoholic has jaundice, ascites, asterixis, and dilated abdominal veins. Labs show elevated bilirubin, low albumin, and elevated INR. Diagnosis?
rationale: Decompensated cirrhosis: jaundice + ascites + encephalopathy (asterixis) + caput medusae. Child-Pugh or MELD score guides management. Tx: diuretics, lactulose, beta-blockers, consider transplant.

caseText: A 25-year-old has recurrent RLQ pain, diarrhea, weight loss, and perianal fistulas. Colonoscopy shows skip lesions and cobblestone mucosa involving the terminal ileum. Diagnosis?
rationale: Crohn's: transmural inflammation + skip lesions + cobblestoning + perianal disease + any GI segment (mouth to anus). UC: continuous mucosal inflammation from rectum upward. Tx: aminosalicylates, steroids, biologics.

caseText: A 72-year-old woman develops sudden right arm weakness, facial droop, and inability to speak for 2 hours. Most appropriate first investigation?
rationale: Acute stroke: CT head NON-CONTRAST first to exclude hemorrhage before tPA. MRI more sensitive but slower. tPA window: 4.5 hours. Door-to-needle <60 min.

caseText: A 50-year-old man has sudden severe headache described as the worst headache of his life while exercising. Neuro exam is normal. Diagnosis?
rationale: Thunderclap headache = SAH until proven otherwise. Dx: CT head first → if negative → LP for xanthochromia. Cause: ruptured berry aneurysm. CT sensitivity decreases after 6 hours.

caseText: What is the single most important modifiable risk factor for stroke?
rationale: Hypertension = #1 modifiable risk factor for stroke. Controlling BP reduces stroke risk by 40%. AF is a major cause of cardioembolic stroke but HTN is overall #1 modifiable factor.

caseText: A 25-year-old man has progressive ascending leg weakness 3 weeks after a GI infection. Reflexes are absent. CSF shows elevated protein with normal WBC. Diagnosis?
rationale: GBS: ascending paralysis + areflexia + albuminocytologic dissociation after infection. Campylobacter #1 trigger. Tx: IVIG or plasmapheresis. Monitor respiratory function.

caseText: A 28-year-old woman has 2 episodes of neurologic deficits separated in time and space: optic neuritis, then 6 months later unilateral limb weakness. MRI shows periventricular white matter lesions. Diagnosis?
rationale: MS: relapsing-remitting neurologic deficits separated in time and space + periventricular white matter lesions. Young women. Dx: MRI + CSF oligoclonal bands. Tx: disease-modifying therapy (interferon, natalizumab).

caseText: A 65-year-old man has resting tremor (pill-rolling), bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, and shuffling gait. Symptoms improve with levodopa. Diagnosis?
rationale: Parkinson's: 4 cardinal signs = TRAP (Tremor at rest, Rigidity cogwheel, Akinesia/bradykinesia, Postural instability). Loss of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra. Tx: levodopa/carbidopa.

caseText: A 20-year-old has fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, and photophobia. Kernig and Brudzinski signs are positive. Most important first step?
options: CT head then LP then IV antibiotics
IV antibiotics + dexamethasone immediately then LP
rationale: Bacterial meningitis: start IV antibiotics + dexamethasone IMMEDIATELY (do not delay for LP or CT if clinical diagnosis clear). Empiric: ceftriaxone + vancomycin + dexamethasone.

caseText: A 30-year-old has recurrent generalized tonic-clonic seizures. First-line treatment for generalized epilepsy?
rationale: Valproic acid: first-line for generalized epilepsy (absence, tonic-clonic, myoclonic). Carbamazepine: first-line for focal seizures. Valproate is teratogenic - use with caution in women of childbearing age.

caseText: A 75-year-old woman has progressive central vision loss and cannot read or recognize faces. Peripheral vision is intact. Diagnosis?
rationale: AMD: central vision loss (macula) in elderly. Dry more common. Wet (neovascular): anti-VEGF injections (ranibizumab, bevacizumab). Risk: age, smoking, family history.

caseText: A 60-year-old man has gradual painless loss of peripheral tunnel vision. IOP is elevated on tonometry. Diagnosis?
rationale: Open-angle glaucoma: increased IOP → optic nerve damage → peripheral tunnel vision loss. Most common type (90%). Tx: prostaglandin eye drops (latanoprost). Acute angle-closure: painful red eye emergency.

caseText: A 45-year-old has polyuria, polydipsia, blurry vision. Random blood glucose is 14.2 mmol/L with symptoms. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: DM diagnosis: random glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L WITH symptoms = diagnostic. Or: fasting ≥7.0, HbA1c ≥6.5%, 2h OGTT ≥11.1 mmol/L (need repeat if asymptomatic).

caseText: Which test is used to both diagnose diabetes AND monitor long-term glucose control?
rationale: HbA1c ≥6.5% = diabetes. Reflects 3-month average glucose. Treatment target: <7%. Cannot use if hemoglobinopathy, hemolytic anemia, or recent transfusion.

caseText: A 52-year-old is newly diagnosed with T2DM, HbA1c 8.2%, no cardiovascular disease. First-line pharmacotherapy?
rationale: Metformin: first-line T2DM. No hypoglycemia, weight neutral, cheap. With established CVD or CKD: add SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 RA for organ protection.

caseText: A 30-year-old woman has weight loss, palpitations, tremor, heat intolerance, diarrhea. Exam shows exophthalmos and diffuse goiter. Diagnosis?
rationale: Graves disease: most common hyperthyroidism. Exophthalmos = pathognomonic. Low TSH + high T3/T4. Tx: Methimazole first-line. Radioactive iodine or surgery as alternatives.

caseText: A 45-year-old woman has fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, and bradycardia. TSH is elevated. Diagnosis and treatment?
rationale: Hypothyroidism: high TSH + low T4. Most common cause: Hashimoto's thyroiditis (autoimmune). Tx: levothyroxine. Myxedema coma: IV T4 + glucocorticoids + ICU.

caseText: A 40-year-old man has central obesity, moon face, buffalo hump, purple abdominal striae, hypertension, and hyperglycemia. Diagnosis?
rationale: Cushing syndrome: excess cortisol. Most common cause: exogenous steroids. Endogenous: ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma (Cushing disease). Screen: 24h urine cortisol or overnight dexamethasone suppression.

caseText: A 19-year-old type 1 diabetic has nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and fruity breath. Blood glucose 28 mmol/L, pH 7.15, bicarbonate 10. Diagnosis?
rationale: DKA: high glucose + anion gap metabolic acidosis + ketonemia. More common in T1DM. Tx: IV fluids first, then insulin infusion, K+ replacement. Bicarbonate only if pH <6.9.

caseText: A 35-year-old has progressive fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, and hyperpigmentation of skin creases and buccal mucosa. Na 128, K 5.8. Diagnosis?
rationale: Addison disease: primary adrenal insufficiency. Hyperpigmentation from high ACTH. Hyponatremia + hyperkalemia. Dx: ACTH stimulation test. Tx: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone.

caseText: A 19-year-old has severe sore throat, fever 39°C, white tonsillar exudate, no cough. Rapid strep test positive. First-line treatment?
rationale: Penicillin V x10 days = gold standard for GAS pharyngitis. Goal: prevent acute rheumatic fever. Amoxicillin acceptable. Azithromycin if penicillin-allergic.

caseText: A 38-year-old immigrant has 4-week cough, fever, night sweats, and 10 kg weight loss. CXR shows upper lobe cavitary lesion. Diagnosis?
rationale: TB: cough >3 weeks + constitutional symptoms + upper lobe cavity. Dx: sputum AFB x3 + culture. Tx: RIPE x2 months then RI x4 months. Isolate until smear-negative.

caseText: A 20-year-old has fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia. LP shows WBC 2000 (PMN predominant), low glucose, high protein. Best empiric antibiotic?
rationale: Bacterial meningitis empiric: ceftriaxone + vancomycin (for penicillin-resistant Strep pneumo) + dexamethasone (reduces neurologic complications). Add ampicillin if >50 years for Listeria.

caseText: A 35-year-old is newly diagnosed with HIV. CD4 count is 180 cells/mm3. What is the most appropriate management?
rationale: ART started immediately regardless of CD4. CD4 <200 → PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX). CD4 <50 → MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin). CD4 <100 → Toxoplasmosis prophylaxis (TMP-SMX covers both).

caseText: A 25-year-old woman has dysuria, frequency, and urgency. Urinalysis shows pyuria and bacteriuria. No fever or flank pain. Most appropriate treatment?
rationale: Uncomplicated UTI: oral nitrofurantoin x5 days or TMP-SMX x3 days (if local resistance <20%). No imaging needed. Complicated UTI (male, pregnant, fever, flank pain): fluoroquinolone x7-14 days.

caseText: A 35-year-old IV drug user has fever, new murmur, Janeway lesions, and Osler nodes. Blood cultures positive for Staphylococcus aureus. Diagnosis?
rationale: Infective endocarditis: Duke criteria. IV drug users: right-sided (tricuspid). Janeway = painless, palms/soles. Osler = painful, fingers/toes. Tx: IV antibiotics x4-6 weeks. MSSA: nafcillin. MRSA: vancomycin.

caseText: A 70-year-old with COPD presents with sudden fever 39.5°C, myalgias, headache, and dry cough 24 hours ago. Rapid influenza test positive. Treatment?
rationale: Influenza: oseltamivir x5 days if started within 48 hours. Benefits high-risk patients (elderly, immunocompromised, chronic disease). Annual vaccination is most effective prevention.

caseText: A 4-year-old child has barking seal-like cough, hoarseness, and inspiratory stridor worse at night. Low-grade fever. First-line treatment?
rationale: Croup (parainfluenza virus): barking cough + stridor. Oral dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg) for ALL severity. Add racemic epinephrine nebulization for moderate-severe. Do not examine throat if epiglottitis suspected.

caseText: A 5-year-old has high fever, drooling, dysphagia, tripod position, and looks toxic. Inspiratory stridor present. Diagnosis?
rationale: Epiglottitis: drooling + dysphagia + tripod + toxic = AIRWAY EMERGENCY. Secure airway FIRST (do NOT examine throat). IV ceftriaxone after airway secured. HiB vaccine prevents most cases.

caseText: A 6-month-old infant has low-grade fever, rhinorrhea, and respiratory distress with expiratory wheezing and crackles. O2 sat 91%. Diagnosis?
rationale: Bronchiolitis: RSV #1 cause. Infants <2 years. Treatment: supportive (O2, hydration, nasal suction). NO benefit from bronchodilators or steroids. Palivizumab for high-risk premature infants.

caseText: A 4-week-old male infant has projectile non-bilious vomiting after every feeding. He appears hungry after vomiting. Olive-shaped mass in epigastrium. Diagnosis?
rationale: Pyloric stenosis: projectile non-bilious vomiting at 2-6 weeks + olive mass + hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis. First-born males. Dx: Ultrasound. Tx: Ramstedt pyloromyotomy.

caseText: A 9-month-old infant has episodic severe crampy abdominal pain, draws up legs, vomiting, and currant jelly stool. Abdomen distended. Diagnosis?
rationale: Intussusception: colicky pain + vomiting + currant jelly stool. Age 6mo-3yr. Dx: Ultrasound (target sign). Tx: air enema reduction (85% success); surgery if failed or perforated.

caseText: A 3-year-old has fever >5 days, bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, polymorphous rash, and swollen hands/feet. Diagnosis?
rationale: Kawasaki: fever >5 days + 4/5 CRASH criteria. Tx: IVIG + aspirin. Most feared complication: coronary artery aneurysm. Screen with echocardiography at diagnosis and 6-8 weeks later.

caseText: A 3-day-old term infant has jaundice. Total bilirubin is 220 μmol/L. The infant is feeding well and appears healthy. Most appropriate management?
rationale: Physiological neonatal jaundice peaks day 3-5. Phototherapy if bilirubin above treatment threshold (based on age and risk factors). Exchange transfusion if severe. Ensure adequate feeding.

caseText: A 2-year-old has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes during a fever of 39.5°C. This is his first seizure. Most appropriate management?
rationale: Simple febrile seizure: brief (<15 min), generalized, single episode. Excellent prognosis. NO anticonvulsant prophylaxis needed. Educate parents. LP if <12 months or signs of meningitis.

caseText: A 3-year-old boy has limited eye contact, no pointing, delayed speech (3 words only), repetitive hand-flapping, and prefers to play alone. Parents noticed regression at 18 months. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: ASD: social communication deficits + restricted repetitive behaviors + onset in early childhood. Screen with M-CHAT at 18 and 24 months. Dx: clinical assessment + developmental team. Tx: ABA therapy, speech therapy.

caseText: A 2-month-old infant with no contraindications. Which vaccines are recommended at this visit?
options: DTaP, IPV, HepB, Hib, PCV13, rotavirus
rationale: 2-month vaccines: DTaP + IPV + HepB + Hib + PCV13 + rotavirus. MMR and varicella: 12-15 months. Meningococcal + HPV: 11-12 years.

caseText: A 10-year-old has polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, and fruity breath. Blood glucose is 28 mmol/L, pH 7.20, bicarbonate 12. Most appropriate management?
options: IV fluid resuscitation then insulin infusion
rationale: Pediatric DKA: aggressive IV fluid resuscitation first, then insulin infusion. Monitor K+ closely (falls with insulin). Avoid rapid correction (risk cerebral edema in children). Hospitalize all pediatric DKA.

caseText: A newborn has cyanosis, decreased pulmonary vascular markings on CXR, and an egg-on-a-string cardiac silhouette. Diagnosis?
rationale: Transposition of great arteries: egg-on-string CXR + cyanosis + decreased pulmonary markings. Incompatible with life without mixing (PDA, VSD, ASD). Emergency: prostaglandin E1 to keep PDA open, then Jatene procedure.

caseText: A 6-month-old has fever 39.8°C, bulging fontanelle, poor feeding, and inconsolable crying. Diagnosis?
rationale: Bacterial meningitis in infants: bulging fontanelle + fever + irritability. Classic triad (headache, neck stiffness, fever) may be absent in infants. Treat empirically: ceftriaxone + ampicillin (Listeria). Do not delay treatment.

caseText: A 14-month-old exclusively drinking cow's milk has pallor, irritability, and hemoglobin of 80 g/L. MCV is low. Diagnosis?
rationale: Iron deficiency anemia: #1 nutritional deficiency in children. Cow's milk low in iron + inhibits absorption. Low Hgb + low MCV + low ferritin. Tx: oral iron + introduce iron-rich foods. Limit cow's milk to 500 mL/day.

caseText: A 7-year-old has recurrent episodes of wheezing, cough (especially at night), and dyspnea triggered by exercise and cold air. Spirometry shows reversible obstruction. Diagnosis and controller therapy?
rationale: Pediatric asthma: recurrent reversible wheeze + atopic history. Mild persistent: low-dose ICS (first-line controller). SABA for rescue. Step up therapy as needed. Spirometry with bronchodilator reversibility confirms Dx.

caseText: A 35-year-old woman has persistent low mood, anhedonia, poor sleep, fatigue, and difficulty concentrating for 6 weeks. She denies suicidal ideation. First-line treatment?
rationale: MDD first-line: SSRIs. Allow 4-6 weeks for response. Treat minimum 6-9 months after remission. CBT + SSRIs superior to either alone. ECT for severe or treatment-resistant MDD.

caseText: A 20-year-old man has 8-month history of auditory hallucinations, believing he is being controlled by aliens, social withdrawal, and flat affect. Diagnosis?
rationale: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions) + negative symptoms (flat affect, withdrawal) >6 months. Tx: atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine). Clozapine for treatment-resistant cases.

caseText: A 28-year-old has recurrent unexpected panic attacks with palpitations and fear of dying. Cardiac workup is negative. First-line long-term treatment?
rationale: Panic disorder: SSRIs + CBT long-term. Benzodiazepines: SHORT-TERM acute attacks only (dependence risk). Beta-blockers: performance anxiety (situational). Interoceptive exposure therapy is key in CBT.

caseText: A patient in the ER has a severe acute panic attack with trembling and palpitations. Fastest-acting treatment?
rationale: Benzodiazepines (lorazepam, diazepam) = fastest relief of acute panic (within minutes). For long-term prevention: SSRI + CBT. Avoid chronic benzodiazepines (tolerance, dependence, withdrawal).

caseText: A 25-year-old man has periods of elevated mood, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, and impulsivity lasting 7 days, alternating with depressive episodes. Diagnosis and first-line treatment?
options: Bipolar I disorder - lithium or valproate
rationale: Bipolar I: manic episodes ≥7 days (or any duration if hospitalized) + major depressive episodes. Tx: lithium (first-line, reduces suicide) or valproate. Avoid antidepressants alone (can trigger mania).

caseText: A chronic alcoholic stopped drinking 24 hours ago. He now has tremors, diaphoresis, tachycardia, hypertension, and anxiety. What is the most feared complication?
rationale: Alcohol withdrawal: seizures peak at 24-48h. DTs peak at 48-72h (confusion, autonomic instability, hallucinations). Tx: IV/oral benzodiazepines (diazepam/lorazepam) + thiamine BEFORE glucose.

caseText: Which patient has the HIGHEST suicide risk?
options: 65-year-old divorced man with previous attempt, access to firearms, and current plan
35-year-old woman with depression but no plan
18-year-old with impulsive behavior but no plan
rationale: Highest risk: elderly + male + divorced/widowed + previous attempt + access to lethal means + specific plan. SAD PERSONS scale. Immediate hospitalization + remove access to means.

caseText: A 32-week pregnant woman has painful vaginal bleeding and a rigid tender uterus. Fetal heart rate is abnormal. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Placental abruption: PAINFUL bleeding + rigid uterus + fetal distress. Risk: HTN, trauma, cocaine. Tx: immediate delivery if fetal distress. IV access + blood products ready.

caseText: A 30-week pregnant woman has painless bright red vaginal bleeding. No contractions or trauma. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Placenta previa: PAINLESS bright red bleeding. Placenta covers cervical os. Do NOT do digital vaginal exam. Dx: Ultrasound. Tx: bed rest if stable; C-section if severe bleeding or at term.

caseText: A 28-week pregnant woman has BP 162/108 mmHg, proteinuria 3+, severe headache, and visual disturbances. Diagnosis and immediate management?
rationale: Severe preeclampsia: BP ≥160/110 + severe features. Tx: hospitalization + MgSO4 (seizure prophylaxis) + IV labetalol or hydralazine + corticosteroids for lung maturity. Delivery at 37 weeks if stable.

caseText: A 25-year-old woman has left lower quadrant pain, vaginal bleeding, and amenorrhea for 7 weeks. BP is 90/60 mmHg and she is diaphoretic. β-hCG is 3500 IU/L. Diagnosis?
rationale: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy: hemodynamic instability + positive β-hCG + no intrauterine pregnancy on US. Risk: PID, prior ectopic, IUD. Tx: EMERGENCY surgery. Unruptured: methotrexate if eligible.

caseText: A 28-week pregnant woman has a 2-hour 75g OGTT glucose of 8.5 mmol/L, diagnostic for GDM. No symptoms. First-line treatment?
rationale: GDM first-line: dietary modification + self-monitoring of blood glucose. If targets not met → insulin (preferred over oral agents in pregnancy). Monitor for macrosomia, birth trauma.

caseText: A 35-year-old woman asks about cervical cancer screening. What is the recommended Canadian approach?
options: Pap smear every 3 years from age 21-69
No screening needed if vaccinated against HPV
rationale: Canadian guidelines: Pap smear every 3 years for women age 21-69. HPV co-testing available. Colposcopy for high-grade lesions or persistent low-grade. HPV vaccine does NOT eliminate screening need.

caseText: A 25-year-old woman has sudden severe right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and vomiting. Ultrasound shows a 7 cm right ovarian cyst with absent Doppler flow. Diagnosis?
rationale: Ovarian torsion: sudden severe unilateral pain + ovarian cyst + absent blood flow on Doppler. SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Time-sensitive: laparoscopy to detorse and preserve ovary if viable.

caseText: A 28-year-old woman has dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain worsening with menses. CA-125 is mildly elevated. Pelvic exam shows fixed retroverted uterus. Diagnosis?
rationale: Endometriosis: dysmenorrhea + dyspareunia + chronic pelvic pain + CA-125 elevated. Gold standard Dx: laparoscopy with biopsy. Tx: NSAIDs + OCP (medical), or surgical excision for severe cases.

caseText: A competent 55-year-old Jehovah's Witness refuses a blood transfusion needed for surgery. He understands he may die. Most appropriate action?
rationale: Competent adult autonomy: right to refuse ANY treatment including life-saving. Document capacity assessment. Explore alternatives (cell salvage, erythropoietin). Family CANNOT override capable adult.

caseText: A physician accidentally prescribes the wrong dose. The patient takes it but suffers no harm. What is the physician's obligation?
options: No disclosure needed since no harm
rationale: Canadian ethics: full disclosure of adverse events AND near-misses is mandatory even if no harm. Apology is not legal admission of liability (Ontario Apology Act). Transparency builds trust.

caseText: A 70-year-old with dementia refuses hip surgery needed after a fall. His daughter wants surgery done. What determines if he can refuse?
rationale: Capacity: patient must understand information, appreciate consequences, reason about decision, and communicate choice. Capacity is decision-specific. If lacking capacity → substitute decision maker (SDM). Dementia ≠ automatically incapable.

caseText: A patient tells you he plans to kill his ex-wife tonight and has a gun. What is your obligation?
options: Warn the intended victim and notify police
rationale: Duty to warn (Tarasoff doctrine): confidentiality is breached when there is serious + imminent + identifiable threat. Must warn identifiable victim AND notify police. Document actions taken.

caseText: A 52-year-old woman who has smoked 1 pack/day for 30 years asks about cancer screening. What is recommended?
rationale: Annual low-dose CT: age 50-80, ≥20 pack-years, currently smoking or quit <15 years. Reduces lung cancer mortality ~20%. CXR is NOT adequate for lung cancer screening.

caseText: A 22-year-old male has sudden severe testicular pain while playing soccer. Testicle is high-riding and cremasteric reflex is absent. Next best step?
rationale: Testicular torsion = surgical emergency. Do NOT delay for imaging if clinical suspicion is high. 6-hour window for salvage. Absent cremasteric reflex = most reliable sign.

caseText: A 65-year-old with prior abdominal surgery has colicky abdominal pain, distension, and vomiting. AXR shows dilated loops of small bowel with air-fluid levels. Diagnosis?
rationale: Small bowel obstruction: colicky pain + distension + vomiting + dilated loops on AXR. #1 cause: adhesions. Tx: NPO + NG tube + IV fluids. Surgery if no improvement or strangulation suspected.

caseText: A 70-year-old man has a 6.5 cm abdominal aortic aneurysm discovered incidentally. He is asymptomatic. Most appropriate management?
rationale: AAA ≥5.5 cm (men) or ≥5.0 cm (women) = elective repair. Elective repair has much better outcomes than emergency surgery for rupture. Options: open surgery or EVAR.

caseText: A 30-year-old involved in MVA is hemodynamically unstable with abdominal tenderness. FAST ultrasound shows free fluid. Most appropriate management?
rationale: Hemodynamically unstable + positive FAST = immediate exploratory laparotomy. DO NOT delay for CT in unstable patients. Stable patients with free fluid → CT for characterization.

caseText: An 80-year-old woman with osteoporosis falls and has hip pain. X-ray shows a displaced femoral neck fracture. Most appropriate treatment?
rationale: Displaced femoral neck fracture in elderly: total hip arthroplasty (THA) is preferred. Lower non-union rate than internal fixation. Early mobilization reduces complications (DVT, pneumonia, pressure sores).

caseText: A 70-year-old with AF has sudden severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam, nausea, and vomiting. WBC is 18,000. Diagnosis?
rationale: Mesenteric ischemia: pain out of proportion to exam in elderly with AF (embolic). Embolic from AF = #1 cause. Dx: CT angiography. Tx: anticoagulation + urgent revascularization or bowel resection.

caseText: A 50-year-old on NSAIDs has sudden onset severe generalized abdominal pain. Exam shows board-like rigidity. CXR shows free air under the diaphragm. Most appropriate management?
rationale: Perforated peptic ulcer: sudden severe pain + peritonism + free air on CXR = SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Emergency laparotomy for repair. IV fluids + antibiotics + PPI preoperatively.

caseText: A 60-year-old has rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits, and 10 kg weight loss. Colonoscopy shows a mass at 15 cm. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma. Stage III (lymph node involvement). Treatment?
rationale: Stage III colorectal cancer: surgery + adjuvant FOLFOX chemotherapy. Rectal cancer: neoadjuvant chemoradiation before surgery. Surgery alone insufficient for stage III. Adjuvant chemo improves survival.

caseText: A 65-year-old male smoker presents with painless hematuria. No dysuria or fever. Urine culture negative. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Painless hematuria in older smoker = bladder cancer until proven otherwise. Transitional cell carcinoma #1. Smoking = major risk factor. Dx: cystoscopy + urine cytology. Non-invasive: BCG. Invasive: radical cystectomy.

caseText: A 45-year-old man has a groin bulge that appears with straining and reduces with lying down. The bulge is medial to the inferior epigastric vessels. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Direct inguinal hernia: medial to inferior epigastric vessels (Hesselbach triangle). More common in older men. Indirect: lateral to epigastric vessels (congenital, through internal ring). Tx: surgical repair (Lichtenstein mesh).
